items.mistake: Not committing poetry.lock to version control.
items.fix: Commit poetry.lock so everyone installs the exact same versions.
items.why: Without it, teammates and CI re-resolve and may get different versions.
items.example: If `.gitignore` lists `poetry.lock`, remove it. The lock is the source of truth — commit and review it like code.

items.mistake: Editing pyproject.toml by hand and forgetting to re-lock.
items.fix: Run poetry lock (or use poetry add) so the lock matches the manifest.
items.why: An out-of-date lock makes poetry install inconsistent with your intended deps.
items.example: Edited `httpx = '^0.27'` → `'^0.28'` manually? Run `poetry lock --no-update` (or `poetry lock`); otherwise CI installs the old 0.27 from the unchanged lock.

items.mistake: Mixing manual pip install into a Poetry-managed env.
items.fix: Use poetry add so the manifest and lock stay authoritative.
items.why: pip-installed packages aren't tracked and break reproducibility.
items.example: Don't: `poetry shell && pip install redis`. Do: `poetry add redis`. The first leaves `redis` in your local env but absent from `pyproject.toml`/`poetry.lock`; CI won't install it.

items.mistake: Putting test/lint tools in the main dependencies.
items.fix: Use poetry add --group dev for them.
items.why: Keeps production installs lean and correct.
items.example: `poetry add pytest mypy ruff` puts them in main deps — every production install pulls them. `poetry add --group dev pytest mypy ruff` keeps them dev-only; prod uses `poetry install --without dev`.
